# Figure description: скв_148.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log typical of Soviet-era geological exploration. It displays multiple geophysical logging curves recorded along the length of a drilled borehole, combined with a lithological column (geological section). This is a standard format used in Soviet mineral and petroleum exploration, printed on long continuous paper strips and subsequently folded or rolled.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log appears to cover a **single borehole (скважина)**, though the borehole number is partially illegible at the top — it may read something like **скв. [illegible]**. The header area (top of the sheet) contains the well identification, location information, and survey metadata typical of Soviet карота́жные диаграммы from Kazakhstan exploration programs of the early 1980s.

The log runs continuously from near the surface down to what appears to be a considerable depth — based on the physical length of the sheet and typical scales used in regional surveys of this type, likely in the range of **several hundred to over 1,000 metres**. The depth scale appears to run top-to-bottom in increments that are partially legible; individual depth markers are visible at regular intervals along the left margin, but specific values are [illegible] due to scan quality.

A **separate inset panel** is visible at the bottom of the sheet, showing an abbreviated repeat or summary section of some of the curves — possibly a **repeat section** or a **different logging run** over a specific interval of interest.

---

## Key Content

The composite log contains multiple vertical columns, which from left to right appear to include:

1. **Depth column (глубина)** — leftmost ruled column with depth markings at regular intervals (likely every 10 or 20 m).

2. **Lithological column (литологическая колонка)** — a narrow column showing symbolic representations of rock types encountered. Visible symbols suggest alternating intervals of:
   - Dotted patterns consistent with **sandstone or sandy sediments**
   - Horizontal-line patterns consistent with **clay or shale (argillite)**
   - Irregular or brecciated patterns possibly indicating **fractured or altered rock**
   - Denser, more uniform fill possibly indicating **dense crystalline or carbonate rock**
   The lithological column changes character noticeably in the lower portion of the log, suggesting a transition to a different lithological suite (possibly basement or volcanogenic sequence).

3. **Spontaneous Potential (СП — самопроизвольная поляризация)** curve — a smoothly varying curve typical of SP logs, deflecting left and right from a baseline. Deflections are visible throughout, with more pronounced anomalies in certain intervals suggesting permeable or porous horizons.

4. **Resistivity (КС — кажущееся сопротивление)** curve(s) — one or more curves showing electrical resistivity variations. These show significant variability, with high-amplitude deflections in certain zones that may indicate resistive (e.g., carbonate, crystalline, or mineralized) layers, and low-resistivity zones corresponding to clays or water-saturated sediments.

5. **Gamma Ray (ГК — гамма-каротаж)** curve — visible as a somewhat irregular curve tracking radioactivity of the formations. Elevated values (rightward deflections) likely correspond to clay-rich or potassic intervals; suppressed values to cleaner sands or carbonates.

6. **Additional curves** — possibly **neutron logging (НК)**, **gamma-gamma (ГГК)**, or **induction log (ИК)** curves occupy further columns to the right, though these are partially obscured by the scan quality. Some curves in the right-hand portion of the upper section of the log appear to terminate partway down (possibly indicating the depth limit of certain tool runs).

7. **Core/sample data columns** — narrow columns to the far right appear to contain tabular data, possibly **core recovery (выход керна)**, sample numbers, or analytical results, represented as dot patterns or numerical entries, but these are [illegible] at this resolution.

**Notable features:**
- There is a visually distinct **zone approximately mid-log** where curve character changes significantly — curves become more chaotic or high-amplitude — possibly indicating a **lithological boundary** or a zone of **intense alteration or mineralization**.
- The **lower portion** of the main log (before the gap leading to the bottom inset) shows somewhat calmer curve behavior, then the log apparently **terminates or is interrupted**, with the bottom inset showing a short interval with very high-amplitude, irregular deflections on all curves — this could represent a **zone of particular interest** (e.g., ore-bearing interval, fractured zone, or basement contact).
- The **inset panel at the bottom** covers what appears to be a relatively short depth interval and shows three to four curves with pronounced, high-frequency variations — suggesting this is a **detail section** of a specific target horizon.
